high.label: Optimistic
high.description: Strong spring push + major gift closes (+25%). Rady -$1M.

knownAdjustments.description: Declined annual fund gift for FY26. Redirecting to Israel campaign. Already reflected in YTD — no additional subtraction.
knownAdjustments.note: Impact is vs FY25, not vs FY26 YTD. His absence is already in the $6.14M.

fy27.baselineNote: FY26 base case projection used as FY27 starting point

low.label: Decline
low.description: 10% erosion — continued attrition, soft economy, no major new donors.

medium.label: Hold Steady
medium.description: Flat year — retention + new acquisition offset losses. Hold the line.

high.label: Growth
high.description: 10% growth — strong spring campaign, pipeline wins, donor upgrades.

knownAdjustments.description: Israel campaign preference assumed to continue. Already absent from FY26 base — no additional subtraction. Flag for DRM: potential re-engagement opportunity.
knownAdjustments.note: His absence is baked into FY26 projected base. Same monotonic rule.

variables.name: FY24 Recognition
variables.note: All accounts. Anomalously high — includes one-time major gifts.

variables.name: FY25 Recognition
variables.note: All accounts. Best comparable baseline (Person $8.8M + Org $2.7M).

variables.name: Donor Retention
variables.note: Count-based. Revenue retention is higher (~85-90%) because major donors retain better.

variables.name: Org Gifts
variables.note: 50 organizations. Lumpy — one grant can swing ±$500K.

variables.name: Spring Major Gifts
variables.note: Apr-Jun is peak solicitation. A single $500K close jumps scenarios.

skepticsNotes: Already at $6,142,437 (87% of goal). Even conservative scenario should land above $8M.
The $9M goal may have assumed Rady. Effective goal without him: $8M — which we're on track to exceed.
Organization gifts ($1.86M from 50 orgs) are lumpy. One large foundation = big swing either direction.
FY27 is pure projection — retention rates, economic conditions, and Israel campaign duration all uncertain.
Revenue retention ≠ donor retention. Top 20 donors account for ~60% of total; they retain at 90%+.
Recognition ≠ cash. $500K pledge counts now, cash arrives over years. See Cash Forecast dashboard.

narrative.title: Why $9M Is Likely Out of Reach — And Why That's Not a Crisis

sections.heading: The Rady Effect
sections.body: FY24 recognition was $15,332,362 — an anomalous high driven by one or two mega-gifts that didn't repeat. FY25 normalized to $8.8M. FY26 is tracking to ~$7.2M. This isn't a freefall — it's a correction from an outlier year. The FY25-to-FY26 decline is ~18%, which is meaningful but not catastrophic.
sections.dataPoint: Ernest Rady redirected his $1M annual fund gift to an Israel campaign. When a single donor represents 11% of a $9M goal and redirects, that's a structural shift, not a performance failure.

sections.heading: Retention Is Actually Fine
sections.body: 53.6% donor retention rate is above the AFP national average of 45%. The Federation is outperforming its peers on retention. The nuance: donor count retention and dollar retention are different. The top 20 donors likely account for 60%+ of total recognition and retain at 90%+. Mid-tier and small donors churn at higher rates — high count, lower dollar impact.
sections.dataPoint: 500 silent donors represent $2.2M at risk, but 97 critical-tier donors account for $1.86M of that. The risk is concentrated, not distributed.

sections.heading: The Concentration Problem
sections.body: The $9M goal likely assumed Rady's $1M continuing and FY24-level major gifts repeating. Neither happened. When one donor shifting priorities creates an 11% gap, the real issue isn't this year's campaign — it's portfolio concentration. You can't replace Rady with 200 new $5K donors (you'd need 200 net-new just to cover him). The path forward is cultivating 3-5 donors in the $250K-$500K range.
sections.dataPoint: To hit $9M from the current $6,142,437, the campaign would need $2,857,563 more — a 47% increase in the remaining 4 months. Historical H2 growth is 10-17%. The math doesn't work without a major gift close.

sections.heading: What the Scenarios Tell Us
sections.body: Conservative (+10% H2): $6,756,681 — minimal new commitments, most recognition already booked. Base case (+17% H2): $7,186,651 — follows FY25 spring pattern. Optimistic (+25% H2): $7,678,046 — strong spring push with major gift closes. All three scenarios land between 75-85% of the $9M goal. The question isn't 'will we hit $9M' — it's 'is $9M the right goal?'
sections.dataPoint: FY27 projections (based on FY26 base) range from $6.5M to $7.9M. A $9.5M FY27 goal would require 32% growth over the best FY26 scenario. Goal-setting needs to reflect the post-Rady, post-FY24 donor base reality.

sections.heading: The Bottom Line
sections.body: The underlying donor program is performing reasonably well. Retention is above industry average. The gap to goal is structural — driven by major donor concentration and a one-time FY24 anomaly that inflated expectations. A realistic goal based on the current donor base is $7-8M. The board conversation should be: 'How do we grow the $250K+ pipeline?' — not 'Why are we underperforming?'
sections.dataPoint: Recommended realistic FY26 target: $7.0-7.5M. Recommended FY27 goal range: $7.0-8.0M (flat to 10% growth). Stretch goal requiring identified pipeline: $8.5M+.
